# Original contribution

# Distinct histopathology of acute onset or abrupt exacerbation of hypersensitivity pneumonitis

Lida P. Hariri MD, PhD a,1 , Mari Mino-Kenudson MD a,⁎,1, Barry Shea MD b, Subba Digumarthy MD c , Maristela Onozato MD, PhD a, Yukako Yagi PhD a, Armando E. Fraire MD d , Osamu Matsubara MD e , Eugene J. Mark MD a

Received 19 March 2011; revised 30 May 2011; accepted 1 June 2011

# Keywords:

Hypersensitivity pneumonitis; Extrinsic allergic alveolitis; Acute; Interstitial lung disease; Acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia; Fibrin

# Summary

Hypersensitivity pneumonitis is an inflammatory lung disease that develops in response to exposure to antigen. Cases can be stratified by the duration of exposure and speed of symptom progression into acute, subacute, and chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Although the pathologic features of subacute hypersensitivity pneumonitis are well established and those of chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis have been reported, little is known about the histopathology of acute hypersensitivity pneumonitis. We evaluated the pathologic features of 5 patients with clinically confirmed hypersensitivity pneumonitis and rapid onset of symptoms and 3 patients with subacute or chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis with symptom exacerbation. Histopathologic features assessed in each case included those characteristic of subacute hypersensitivity pneumonitis (bronchiolocentric chronic inflammation, histiocytic aggregates, and bronchiolitis obliterans), those associated with acute inflammation (fibrin deposition and neutrophilic infiltrate), and fibrosis. The classic features of hypersensitivity pneumonitis were identified in all 8 cases, with 1 also exhibiting fixed fibrosis confirming underlying chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Fibrin deposition was present in 8 (100%) of 8 cases, and its extent was significant (28% surface area fibrin deposition/total disease area on average). Two had intra-alveolar fibrin so marked that it resembled acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia. In addition, prominent interstitial neutrophilic infiltrate (≥5 cells/high-power field) was seen in all cases. These features have not been reported as characteristics of subacute or chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Increased fibrin deposition and neutrophilic infiltrate may characterize acute hypersensitivity pneumonitis or abrupt exacerbation of hypersensitivity pneumonitis, and these along with characteristic features of subacute hypersensitivity pneumonitis (granulomatous inflammation and bronchiolocentricity) are sufficient to establish a morphologic diagnosis, particularly in conjunction with clinicoradiologic features.

pages.md:
Acute hypersensitivity pneumonitis

# 1. Introduction

Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HSP) is a form of inflammatory lung disease, characterized by an inflammatory response involving the small airways, alveoli, and interstitium in response to an inhaled antigen, particularly from mold or birds. Clinically, it can be categorized into 3 forms, acute, subacute, and chronic, which are distinguished by duration of exposure to the antigen, interval between exposure to onset of symptoms, duration of symptoms, and clinical presentation [1-6].

Subacute and chronic HSPs occur in response to repetitive exposure to inhaled antigen, presenting with an insidious onset of dyspnea lasting a few weeks to months with associated radiologic and pathologic evidence of interstitial lung disease. The pathologic features of subacute HSP have been well defined in the literature, consisting of a bronchiolocentric chronic inflammatory infiltrate with associated loosely formed histiocyte aggregates and/or giant cells [1-3]. Chronic HSP has been characterized by the same pathologic features as subacute HSP with the addition of a fibrosing interstitial pneumonitis, which has been correlated with a worse prognosis. Many patterns of fibrosis have been described, including prominent peribronchiolar fibrosis, usual interstitial pneumonitis–like pattern, and nonspecific interstitial pneumonitis–like fibrosis [7-14].

Patients with acute HSP classically present with the abrupt onset of fever, chills, and cough of several hours' duration, typically peaking 6 to 23 hours after an acute exposure to large quantities of antigen. Symptoms resolve quickly after avoidance of further exposure to the inciting agent [15]. In the setting of repeated exposure, the diagnosis is usually made within a month after the initial onset. As a result of the rapid recovery, biopsies are rarely performed, and thus, there is little in the literature about the histopathology in acute HSP.

We recently encountered a patient with clinically confirmed HSP who presented with rapid onset of symptoms and had a lung biopsy that showed acute fibrinous and organizing pneumonia (AFOP) along with features of subacute HSP. Prompted by this case, we have analyzed 5 HSP cases (the index case and 4 additional cases) with acute onset of symptoms to identify the histopathologic features of acute HSP. We also studied 3 cases with clinically confirmed subacute or chronic HSP that showed similar histopathologic features and well-documented, abrupt exacerbation of symptoms. These 8 cases formed our study cohort. One of the cases was diagnosed on a transbronchial lung biopsy, and the remainder were diagnosed on wedge biopsies.

# 2. Materials and methods

# 2.1. Case selection

Sixty-four surgical or transbronchial biopsies with a possible clinical and/or pathologic diagnosis of HSP were identified in the pathology database of Massachusetts General Hospital and from the consultations of one of the authors (E.J.M.). Acquisition of the tissue specimens was approved by the Institutional Review Board of Massachusetts General Hospital and performed in accordance with Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act regulations. Upon reviewing the histology and the details of clinical information including symptoms, symptom duration, environmental exposures, radiologic findings, and the results of laboratory tests and an HSP panel in each case, 4 of these cases and the aforementioned index case were diagnosed to have HSP with acute onset symptomatology (acute HSP), based on a combination of clinical symptoms, clinical interpretation of radiologic findings and serology/known antigen exposure, and biopsies that showed a richly cellular process. We also identified 3 additional cases that were clinicopathologically confirmed subacute or chronic HSP with abrupt symptomatic exacerbation and similar histopathologic features as those of the acute HSP cases.

# 2.2. Pathologic features

We evaluated the pathologic features characteristic of HSP (bronchiolocentric chronic inflammatory infiltrate, loose histiocytic aggregates, giant cells, and bronchiolitis obliterans) as well as those possibly indicating an acute process (fibrin and neutrophilic infiltrate). The presence of loose histiocytic aggregates, the extent of fibrin deposition, and organizing and fixed collagen was evaluated semiquantitatively on a scale of 0 to 4. Histiocytic giant cells were semiquantitatively evaluated on a scale of 0 to 2, defined as follows: 0, none; 1, only a few giant cells per slide; and 2, numerous giant cells.

# 1.1. Index case

A 55-year-old woman presented with a 1-week history of an acute onset of cough, shortness of breath, fever, chills, and fatigue shortly after cleaning her recently flooded basement and car. Computer tomography of the chest revealed multifocal consolidation in both peribronchovascular and subpleural distributions with extensive patchy ground glass opacities. The radiologic differential diagnosis included cryptogenic organizing pneumonia, aspiration pneumonia, or multifocal infectious pneumonia, but HSP was suspected based on the patient's clinical history. An HSP panel was performed and showed reactivity to 3 forms of Aspergillus fumigatus. A video-assisted thorascopic wedge biopsy was performed 11 days after the onset of symptoms. Microscopic evaluation revealed regions of AFOP along with features consistent with HSP (Fig. 1). She was treated with corticosteroids. There was also abatement of molds in her home and car. Her symptoms resolved completely.

pages.md:
Acute hypersensitivity pneumonitis

and the presence or absence of bronchiolitis obliterans 8 cases (Figs. 1A-C, 2A-D, 3A-B, and 4A-B), and the extent of fibrin deposition was 28% on average (range, 5%-80%), with a trend toward more prominent fibrin deposition present in acute HSP cases (average, 40%; range, 10%-80%). Two cases (cases 1 and 5) showed intra-alveolar fibrin deposition so marked as to qualify as AFOP (Figs. 1A-C and 2A-D). Edema fluid and scant cellular debris could be seen in some alveoli associated with the fibrin. Vascular ingrowth and prominent fibroblastic proliferation as observed in bronchiolitis obliterans or in chronic organizing pneumonia were not present. Phosphotungstic acid hematoxylin stain showed that most of the substance within alveoli was positive. Trichrome stain did not show collagenous fibrosis amid either organizing inflammation or fibrin in the afflicted alveoli. Interstitial neutrophils were prominent, with an average of 16 neutrophils/HPF (range, 5-25 neutrophils/HPF). Fixed fibrosis was absent in all but 1 case, in which the clinicopathologic diagnosis of chronic HSP with exacerbation was rendered (Table 2).

# 3. Results

# 3.1. Clinical features

Table 1 summarizes the clinical features and exposure characteristics for the 5 cases of acute HSP and 3 cases of HSP with acute HSP-like histology. The average age was 45.9 years (range, 9-68 years). Seven patients were female, and 1 was male. Information on definite or possible sensitizing agents was available for 7 cases. There was exposure to mold in 6 patients and to birds in 1 patient. One patient had no identifiable antigenic exposure. Two patients (cases 3 and 4) also had chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

In the 5 cases of acute onset symptoms, the duration of symptoms before surgical biopsy was 23 ± 14 days (ranging 8 days to 6 weeks). All cases presented with a new onset of shortness of breath, cough, and/or fatigue. Fever and/or chills were also seen in 4 of the 5 cases. Auscultation of the lungs revealed bilateral coarse breath sounds in 2 cases, decreased breath sounds in 1 case, and a clear chest on auscultation in 1 case. The clinical and radiographic features made HSP enter the differential diagnosis in 4 cases, but lung biopsy was performed nonetheless due to a concern for atypical pneumonia. All patients were treated with corticosteroid therapy, and an attempt was made to remove any possible offending antigen. There was complete resolution of symptoms in 4 patients and amelioration of symptoms in 1 patient.

In addition to the above-described cases, 2 cases with subacute HSP and 1 case with chronic HSP showed acute HSP-like histology. Clinically, these patients experienced exacerbation of cough, shortness of breath, and fatigue that eventuated in a lung biopsy. Fever and/or chills were not seen in any of these 3 cases. Auscultation of the lungs revealed bibasilar crackles in 2 cases and bilateral coarse rales in 1 case. One patient was treated with steroid and antigen removal, which resulted in complete symptom resolution. One patient had continued exposure to mold, and her clinical course deteriorated despite corticosteroid treatment. The third patient was lost to follow-up.

# 4. Discussion

Literature on the pathologic features of acute HSP is scant. This is probably due to the rarity of biopsy in patients whose disease usually resolves quickly. Two of the few cases in the literature happen to appear in the same issue of Thorax in 1968 [16,17]. One report [16] had 1 acute and 4 subacute or chronic cases. The other report [17] was a fatal case described as farmer's lung in a 17-year-old where the pathology had features of acute disease as well as granulomatous and organizing inflammation. In addition, Miyazaki and colleagues [18] reported acute exacerbation superimposed on chronic HSP in 14 patients selected from 100 consecutive patients diagnosed to have chronic bird fancier's lung. This might lead to a conclusion that chronic HSP with exacerbation is more readily recognized than acute HSP because there is already a clinical suspicion of HSP. There may also be a greater tendency to biopsy the lung of a patient with subacute or chronic HSP because disease has been perceived but undocumented until confirmed by biopsy, whereas the acute forms of disease were assumed to be infectious pneumonia, toxic inhalation, or other causes of acute lung injury.

In this study, we identify 8 cases of HSP: 5 cases with new and rapid onset of symptoms (symptom duration before biopsy b6 weeks, ranging from 8 days to 6 weeks) and 3 cases with exacerbation of symptoms, likely due to acute, high-dose antigen exposure or reexposure in the setting of subacute or chronic HSP. Biopsies were obtained in these patients because in each case, there was a concern for an atypical pneumonia. All cases were characterized by fibrin deposition (≥5% surface area fibrin deposition/total disease area) and neutrophilic infiltrates (≥5 neutrophils/HPF). Alveolar fibrin and neutrophils could be construed as a form of acute lung injury.

# Acute hypersensitivity pneumonitis

Fig. 2 A case of acute HSP (case 5) exhibiting acute fibrinous organizing pneumonia-like histology. A large number of alveolar spaces are filled with fibrin (A: hematoxylin and eosin stain; B: phosphotungstic acid hematoxylin stain, original magnification ×40, scale bar: 500 μm). High-power magnification demonstrates prominent fibrin deposition in alveoli and interstitial inflammatory infiltrate (C: hematoxylin and eosin stain; D: phosphotungstic acid hematoxylin stain, original magnification ×200, scale bar: 150 μm). The adjacent area shows peribronchiolar mixed inflammatory infiltrate with increased interstitial neutrophils (E: hematoxylin and eosin stain original magnification ×200, scale bar: 150 μm; F: hematoxylin and eosin stain, original magnification ×400, scale bar: 100 μm).

Acute HSP is characterized by lung disease that occurs after an acute exposure to antigen and presents with the abrupt onset of symptoms of several hours' duration. In the setting of repeated exposure, the diagnosis is usually made within 4 weeks from the initial onset of symptoms.

pages.md:
# 666 L. P. Hariri et al.

Fig. 3 An example of acute HSP (case 4) exhibiting a bronchiolocentric distribution of disease process (A: hematoxylin and eosin stain, original magnification ×40, scale bar: 500 μm). Alveolar spaces are expanded by fibrin deposition admixed with inflammatory cells (B: hematoxylin and eosin stain, original magnification ×200, scale bar: 150 μm). Interstitial inflammatory infiltrate and loose histiocytic aggregates (arrows) are seen in the adjacent peribronchiolar region (C: hematoxylin and eosin stain, original magnification ×100, scale bar: 300 μm). There are increased interstitial neutrophils (arrows) (D: hematoxylin and eosin stain, original magnification ×1000 oil, scale bar: 35 μm).

In cases that were classified as acute HSP in this study, the duration of symptoms averaged 23 days and was attributed as a response to new antigenic exposure. These patients displayed acute symptomatology including rapid onset of cough, shortness of breath, fever, and chills. The diagnosis in one case was made more than 4 weeks from the initial onset but had the histopathology similar to the other cases in this study. The 3 cases with clinically confirmed subacute or chronic HSP and acute HSP-like histopathology experienced abrupt exacerbation of symptoms that resulted in a lung biopsy. Although fibrin deposition was not as prominent in these cases as in acute HSP, significant neutrophilic infiltrate in the background of more established chronic components (lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate and/or fixed fibrosis) is consistent with reexposure to high-dose antigen in the setting of subacute or chronic HSP.

HSP can be induced by various allergens including, most commonly, thermophilic actinomyces, other fungal antigens, or antigens from exposure to birds as seen in so-called bird fanciers' pneumonitis. To our knowledge, these 3 types of exposures have not been studied to compare histologic differences. Such analysis may be possible with further study. Clinical features of patients in Japan with so-called summer-type HSP have been studied extensively [23], but

pages.md:
the pathology in these cases is not described as distinctive.

One of our cases was associated with birds and the others with either Aspergillus or thermophilic species.

In summary, HSP with acute clinical onset may have fibrin deposition, which may be so extensive as to resemble or represent AFOP, with prominent interstitial neutrophils. These features in the presence of more classic HSP should raise suspicion for an acute exposure to antigen whether or not there is known subacute or chronic HSP. These features can be useful in identifying patients with current acute and possibly high-dose exposures to antigen so that the exposure can be identified and eliminated promptly.
